coredata.dc:title: Long wavelength emission multicolor fluorescent carbon quantum dots for the detection of pH, amino acids, and metal ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Rui
He, Simin
Liu, Wanjing
Pei, Hebing
Liu, Nijuan
Guo, Ruibin
Mo, Zunli
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots are widely used in the field of analysis and detection due to their excellent optical properties. At present, many studies have focused on the preparation of multi-color fluorescent quantum dots by simple methods. Inspired by this, carbon quantum dots with green (G-CQDs) and yellow fluorescence (YN-CQDs) emission were prepared by hydrothermal method using PTCDA and urea as precursors. After characterizing their morphology, structure, and optical properties, fluorescent probes for different purposes were constructed according to their different fluorescence characteristics. Since green G-CQDs are sensitive to acidic environments, pH detection kits are developed. In addition, G-CQDs can also efficiently detect Asp. YN-CQDs are used to detect metal ions and can specifically detect Fe3+. Both of them were applied to the detection of actual samples, showing high efficiency and reliability. It provides a new idea for the multi-purpose detection of carbon quantum dot fluorescent probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor fluorescent
Fluorescence detection
Aspartic acid
PH
Iron ion
Long Wavelength Emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Asp and Fe3+
Optical properties of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs
Green fluorescent G-CQDs for Asp detection
Yellow fluorescent YN-CQDs for Fe3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) G-CQDs; (b) YN-CQDs, illustration is particle size statistics; TEM image of YN-CQDs with lattice (c) G-CQDs; (d) YN-CQDs.

G-CQDs:(a) XPS survey spectrum; High resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C1s; (c) O1s; (d) FT-IR spectrum of PTCDA, G-CQDs and YN-CQDs; (e) XPS survey spectrum of YN-CQDs; High resolution XPS spectrum of (f) C1s; (g) O1s; (h) N1s.

G-CQDs: (a) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra; (b) Emission spectra; (c) CIE 1931 spectra of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs; YN-CQDs: (d) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra; (e) Emission spectra; (f) UV–vis spectra of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs.
Physical photograph of G-CQDs with pH ranging from 0.25 to 5.00.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs is induced by different types of amino acids:Glu, Cys, Trp, Tyr, Pro, Asp, Met, His, Ile, Lys, Phe, Ser, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs, F is the fluorescence intensity after the amino acids are added; (b) Incubation time relationship between G-CQD and Asp; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-CQDs in the presence of increasing Asp concentrations from 0 to 180 μM; (d) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Asp concentrations from 0 to 180 μM.
G-CQDs and G-CQDs + Asp: (a) Fluorescence lifetime fitting curves; (b) UV–vis spectra.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs is induced by different types of metal ions:Mg2+,Fe2+,Li+,Ca2+,Cd2+,Mn2+,Cu2+,Fe3+,Ni2+,Co2+,Pb2+, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs, F is the fluorescence intensity after the metal ions are added; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of YN-CQDs in the presence of increasing Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 250 μM; (c) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 250 μM; (d) Fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs and interfering metal ions before and after mixed with Fe3+ (The concentration of Fe3+ was 0.1 mM and the concentration of the other interfering ions was 1 mM). F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs and interfering metal ions, F is the fluorescence intensity after the Fe3+ are added.
(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of YN-CQDs + Fe3+ and excitation and emission spectrum of YN-CQDs;(b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of YN-CQDs and the YN-CQDs solution containing Fe3+.
Schematic diagram of preparation strategy of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs based probe.
Comparison of G-CQDs probe and traditional methods for detecting pH.

head.ce:title: Long wavelength emission multicolor fluorescent carbon quantum dots for the detection of pH, amino acids, and metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials
Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials
Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots are widely used in the field of analysis and detection due to their excellent optical properties. At present, many studies have focused on the preparation of multi-color fluorescent quantum dots by simple methods. Inspired by this, carbon quantum dots with green (G-CQDs) and yellow fluorescence (YN-CQDs) emission were prepared by hydrothermal method using PTCDA and urea as precursors. After characterizing their morphology, structure, and optical properties, fluorescent probes for different purposes were constructed according to their different fluorescence characteristics. Since green G-CQDs are sensitive to acidic environments, pH detection kits are developed. In addition, G-CQDs can also efficiently detect Asp. YN-CQDs are used to detect metal ions and can specifically detect Fe3+. Both of them were applied to the detection of actual samples, showing high efficiency and reliability. It provides a new idea for the multi-purpose detection of carbon quantum dot fluorescent probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are important semiconductor nanomaterials, usually spherical or quasi-spherical with a diameter of 1–10 nm, sometimes called “artificial atoms”. Due to its very small size, it has quantum size effect, surface effect, and quantum confinement effect, which also makes CQD have special optical, electrical, and magnetic properties[1]. CQDs are chemically and physically stable, with a large surface-to-mass ratio, and tunable luminescence properties, high fluorescence stability, and significant quantum confinement effect, and are easily dispersed in water due to the functional groups at the edges. These properties make CQDs favored by authoritative researchers in fluorescent probe detection[2,3], biological imaging[4,5], information encryption[6], chemical sensing[7], light-emitting diodes[8,9], etc.

Initially, CQDs focused on segment wavelength emission and generally low quantum yields. However, in recent years, through surface modification or passivation, heteroatom doping, CQDs can achieve long-wavelength emission and quantum yields have also been significantly improved[10]. Among them, heteroatom doping is most favored by researchers. Heteroatom doping is usually divided into metal doping and non-metal doping. Unlike metals, the doping process of non-metallic elements is simple and effective and can be more easily doped into the carbon backbone[11]. It can change the electronic structure of CQDs, improve the surface defects of CQDs and achieve the purpose of modifying CQDs[12].

Rina et al. synthesized green and yellow–red long wavelength emitting CQDs by refluxing the three phenylenediamine isomers with the amino groups at the three inter-neighboring positions in diphenyl ether at 250 °C for 4 h. The highest quantum yield of 15% was obtained, which was subsequently dispersed in different solvents. Subsequent dispersion in different solvents can increase the quantum yield to 36%[13]. Yuan et al. used perylene as a precursor to synthesize 3,4,9,10-nitroperylene-substituted derivatives by adding nitric acid at high temperature, and prepared a CQDs with green–red light reversible switching ability by solvent heat treatment of 3,4,9,10-nitroperylenes in alkaline solution, both with quantum yields up to 80%[14]. Perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA), composed of perylene ring and anhydride groups, is a derivative of perylene, and the conjugated structure ensures its chemical and photostability in different states, allowing the formation of highly crystalline films and dyes, mostly used in electronic and optoelectronic applications, showing great potential for applications in optoelectronic devices. Due to its short intermolecular distance, leading to π-π conjugation, PTCDA polymerization can be considered to extend the conjugation, allowing for long-wave emission production[15]. Meanwhile, PTCDA can be used as a light-absorbing monomer and also for the preparation of 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid aromatic fluorophore dyes for reversible fluorescence conversion on different substrates[16].

In recent years, variety fluorescent nanomaterial sensors have been extensively investigated for Fe3+ and Cys detection due to their convenient, rapid, low cost and a host of other unique features. For instance, Borah 's group developed L-cysteine modified caffeic acid mediated gold nanoparticles (Cys-Caf-AuNP) that selectively detected Fe3+ in the range of 0.5–5 µM with an LOD of 0.22 µM. showed high selectivity and good promise in the detection of Fe3+ [17]. Joseph et al. synthesized a water-soluble decamer derivative of column [5] aromatic hydrocarbon (APA) containing triazole and amide fractions. APA selectively detected Fe3+ (LOD = 689 ppm). FeAPA selectively detected Cys (LOD = 1740 ppm) and detected F- (LOD = 434 ppm) [18].

Combined with the excellent properties of PTCDA, in this part of the work, green luminescent G-CQDs with high quantum yield were prepared by hydrothermal method using PTCDA as precursor and adding an appropriate amount of NaOH, and yellow fluorescent YN-CQDs were prepared by adding an appropriate amount of NaOH and urea. Through TEM, it can be characterized and analyzed by XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis and fluorescence lifetime, which can visually detect pH and specifically detect Asp and Fe3+. The quenching mechanism was obtained by theoretical analysis and practical detection. Subsequently, G-CQDs and YN-CQDs were applied to the detection of actual samples, and the recovery rate was determined.

Amino acids including: Methionine (Met), Histidine (His), and Serine (Ser) were purchased from Macklin Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutamic acid (Glu), Aspartic acid (Asp), Isoleucine (Ile), Proline (Pro) and Tyrosine (Tyr) were purchased from Aladdin Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phenylalanine (Phe) was purchased from Energy Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cysteine (Cys), Lysine (Lys), and Tryptophan (Trp) were purchased from Yuan ye Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O, LiCl, CdCl2·2.5H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, CoCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, PbC4H6O4·3H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained by JEM-3010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy images were performed on FLS1000 transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was performed on FTS-3000 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron energy spectrum (XPS) spectrum was carried out with Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo, USA). The photoluminescence spectrums were recorded on the FluoroMax-4 Fluorescence Spectrometer (HORIBA, USA).

G-CQDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method by adding 0.083 g PTCDA and 15 mL 0.2 M NaOH in 50 mL deionized water and ultrasonically dissolved, YN-CQDs was dissolved by adding 0.083 g PTCDA, 0.083 g urea and 15 mL 0.2 M NaOH in 50 mL deionized water and ultrasonically dissolved in Scheme 1 . They were then transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 120 min. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter paper membrane until no large particles were visible to the naked eye and the entire solution was clarified. The remaining solution was then spin evaporated to about 5 mL, dialyzed in a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, and finally freeze-dried to a powder for subsequent characterization and experiments.

Fluorescent quantum yield refers to the utilization of photons in photochemical reactions and can be defined as the number of molecules produced by each quantum absorbed. There are two commonly used methods to determine QY, one is the relative method, and the other is the absolute method. The measurement of the relative method must rely on reference objects, which limits its applicability[19]. In this experiment, the quantum yield of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs was determined by FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. Firstly, the excitation correction intensity of blank and sample was measured, and then the emission correction intensity of blank and sample was measured. Secondly, the excitation and emission spectra were corrected and the area was calculated by fluorescence spectrometer and integrating sphere. Finally, the QY of G-CQD is 76.98, and the QY of YN-CQD is 20.73 in Fig. S1 . The quantum yield was obtained by averaging three times. This method has the advantages of simple and fast measurement, good repeatability and stability, and small error.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Asp and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mL of 10 μg/mL quantum dot solution was taken as the main body and added to the cuvette, 0–180 μM concentration of Asp and 0–250 μM concentration of Fe3+ were used as the guest. 30 μL of Asp and Fe3+ were added to the respective body solutions and mixed. To detect different types of amino acids and metal ions, their volumes were also controlled at 30 μL and then mixed. In the fluorescence spectrophotometer, the same excitation wavelength was controlled and the tests were performed under uniform conditions.

The urine samples were subjected to a 50-fold dilution to reduce the inference from urine samples and spiked with different concentrations of Asp added[20]. The lake water sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The concentration was calculated according to the standard curve and compared with the actual concentration to calculate the fortified recoveries. Each set of experiments was tested three times.

The morphology of the material can be seen by TEM. The TEM images of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1
(a-b). Both of them are nearly spherical dispersed particles with no agglomeration and narrow size distribution. About 100 nanoparticles were counted to obtain the size distribution in Fig. 1 (c-d), and the average particle size of G-CQDs was 2.23 ± 0.2 nm and that of YN-CQDs was 1.35 ± 0.2 nm. HRTEM images show that G-CQDs and YN-CQDs have highly similar high-resolution lattice fringes. The lattice spacing is 0.22 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, indicating that it has the same or similar structure as graphite. In the Raman spectra of CQDs (Fig. S2), the disorder (D) band observed at 1354.5 cm−1 and 1361.9 cm−1 is caused by the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon. However, the graphite (G) band perceived at 1572 cm−1 is caused by the graphite's E2g mode and the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The intensity of the G band is greater than that of the D band indicating that the CQDs are primarily composed of sp2 graphitic carbons with defects. The above results also show that the prepared YN-CQDs are pure and impurity-free carbon dots[21].

FT-IR can study the structural composition of molecules or determine their chemical groups, and XPS can provide information on molecular structure and atomic valence states. The combination of FT-IR and XPS can analyze the chemical composition of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs. In order to further characterize the functional groups on the surface of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs, the FT-IR spectra of raw PTCDA, G-CQDs and YN-CQDs were compared. Fig. 2
(d) the peaks at 1773 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1 in PTCDA molecule are the vibration peaks of C = O in anhydride, and the peaks at 1301 cm−1 and 1020 cm−1 are the extension of -C-O-C[21,22]. Compared with G-CQDs, the absorption band near 1700 cm−1 disappears due to the R-COO-group produced by ionization. Instead, peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 appear, corresponding to antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration peaks[23]. The introduction of urea in YN-CQDs makes the C = O bonds in YN-CQDs move to lower wavenumbers, which is attributed to the conjugation effect. The second change is the appearance of the C-N bond at 1150 cm−1, the aromatic amine at 1380 cm−1 and the pyridine nitrogen near 1680 cm−1. The appearance of these nitrogen-containing functional group peaks proves that the nitrogen element in urea is successfully doped into the YN-CQDs skeleton[24].

XPS spectra were used to characterize the structural composition of the elements. Fig. 2 (a) is a full scan spectrum of XPS showing that G-CQDs was mainly composed of C, O and Na and YN-CQDs was mainly composed of C, O, N and Na, which is consistent with the raw material, and the added NaOH is involved in the reaction to form sodium carboxylate. The characteristic peaks of G-CQDs at 285.08 eV, 1071.75 eV and 531.08 eV are C 1s, Na 1s and O 1s, respectively, indicating that G-CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, sodium and oxygen. The main peak of C 1 s in Fig. 2 (b) includes three sub-peaks, including 284.88 eV (C-C/C = C), 286.68 eV (C-O) and 288.38 eV (C = O). The main peak of O1s in Fig. 2 (c) includes three sub-peaks, including 530.98 eV (O = C), 532.68 eV (O-C) and 535.08 eV (HO-C/C-O-C)[25,26]. Fig. 2 (e) is the XPS full spectrum of YN-CQDs. The characteristic peaks of YN-CQDs at 287.08 eV, 407.08 eV, 531.08 eV and 1072.08 eV are C-1s, N-1s, O 1 s and Na 1 s. Fig. 2 (f) is the spectrum of C 1s, in which the main peak of C 1s is fitted into four peaks, 284.38 eV, 285.48 eV, 287.08 eV and 288.88 eV, corresponding to C-C/C = C, C-N, C-O and C = O, respectively. Fig. 2 (g) shows the spectra of O 1s, where the main peak of O 1s is fitted. The three peaks are 532.48 eV, 533.78 eV and 537.28 eV, corresponding to O = C, O-C and HO-C/C-O-C, respectively. Fig. 2 (h) is the spectrum of N 1s, in which the main peak of N 1s is fitted into three peaks, 399.68 eV, 400.78 eV and 401.88 eV, respectively, corresponding to pyrrole nitrogen, N-C and amino nitrogen. Combined with XPS and FT-IR spectra, it can be shown that YN-CQDs have a large number of functional groups containing carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. The biggest difference between YN-CQDs and G-CQDs is the appearance of nitrogen, which also proves the successful doping of nitrogen in urea [27–29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The 3D Ex-Em three-dimensional fluorescence images of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3
(a) and Fig. 3 (d). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs increases first and then decreases, with the excitation wavelength from 400 to 470 nm, and the emission wavelength position does not shift, indicating that the emission characteristics are independent of excitation. The most suitable excitation wavelength of G-CQDs is 430 nm and the emission wavelength is 525 nm. The YN-CQDs are three-excitation single-emission, which have excitation peaks at 470 nm, 483 nm and 491 nm, but the strongest intensity at 470 nm is the best excitation wavelength. No matter how Ex changes, Em only peaks at about 585 nm, showing a single emission peak, and the optimal emission wavelength is at 585 nm. Fig. 3 (b) shows the excitation emission curve of G-CQDs. Under the excitation of 430 nm, G-CQDs has a single peak at 525 nm, and the overall emission peaks are in the green band. Fig. 3 (e) is the emission spectrum of YN-CQDs, with a single peak at 585 nm, and the fluorescence is in the range of yellow light. Both of them are consistent with the physical images in the illustrations. In Fig. 3 (c), G-CQDs and YN-CQDs are summarized in the CIE 1931 coordinate diagram. It can be seen that G-CQDs are in the green light area and YN-CQDs are in the yellow light area. Compared with the physical color, the theory and practice are consistent.

The UV–vis spectra of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs were studied in Fig. 3 (f). It can be clearly seen from the UV–vis spectra of G-CQDs that there are three obvious absorption peaks at 263 nm, 437 nm and 466 nm. The strong absorption peak at 263 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of benzene ring, and 437 nm and 466 nm are caused by the n-π* transition of chromophores[30]. In the UV–vis spectra of YN-CQDs, the peaks at 263 nm, 437 nm and 466 nm disappeared and replaced by the peak at 500 nm, which confirmed that the introduction of nitrogen element made the wavelength of YN-CQDs shift to the long wave direction, representing the expansion of the conjugated structure of YN-CQDs, which in turn showed yellow light. Urea is added to YN-CQDs during the preparation process. Urea has nitrogen. The atomic radius, electronegativity and electronic structure of nitrogen are similar to those of carbon, and the functional groups containing nitrogen are more compatible. It is easy to transition from the ground state to the lowest excited state of electrons. Nitrogen contributes its unpaired electrons as electron donors to improve the emission of YN-CQDs[12].

The most important thing of fluorescent probes in practical applications is fluorescence stability, and then the long-term storage stability and salt tolerance of G-CQDs and YN-CQDs were tested. It can be seen from Fig. S3
(a-b) that the fluorescence of the two is very stable and can be maintained for at least 30 days at room temperature. And its fluorescence intensity is basically unchanged, only floating up and down, indicating that it has excellent fluorescence stability. With the increase of NaCl concentration from 0 to 1 M, the effect on the fluorescence intensity of the two is not significant, only about 5% fluctuation in Fig. S3
(c-d). The PL spectra were tested by dispersing the materials into B-R buffers of different pH. As shown in Fig. S4, the composites maintained good PL strength in all environments except for the comparison of bursts in strong acid solutions at pH = 2.0–13.0. It can be seen that CQDs maintain excellent stability even under harsh conditions. Cycling experiments were carried out on them at pH = 2.0 and pH = 9.0 and the probes were found to have good fluorescence burst and recovery. We deduce that the different functional groups attached to the surface of CQDs are responsible for the change in fluorescence intensity and colour upon pH change. The above data show that the two as fluorescent probes have certain potential in the use of practical complex scenes, and have good fluorescence characteristics, low cost and light stability.

In Fig. 4 , it can be seen that G-CQDs are very sensitive to acidic conditions, showing bright green light under alkaline conditions, and obvious quenching occurs pH = 3, and the stronger the acidity, the more obvious the quenching. This is attributed to the serious agglomeration under strong acidic conditions, resulting in larger size of G-CQDs and loss of fluorescence characteristics, which is manifested as fluorescence quenching[31]. Also, based on this phenomenon, it can be used to develop visualization kits for pH detection. The pH test paper is easy to use in real life, but usually not accurate enough, and the pH meter is very accurate but relatively troublesome to use, requiring calibration before use and cleaning and maintenance after use. As can be seen in Table 1 , the color change of the G-CQDs probe for pH detection is obvious, which is easier to identify than pH paper, and the detection interval is narrower, so the G-CQDs can be made into pH test kits for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green fluorescent G-CQDs for Asp detection
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 5
(a), the quenching ability of different amino acids to G-CQDs was studied. It can be seen from the figure that Asp has the strongest fluorescence quenching ability to G-CQDs, indicating that G-CQDs can efficiently detect Asp in aqueous solution. The quenching ability of other 10 amino acids to G-CQDs is not as strong as Asp. Fig. 5 (b) is the incubation time diagram of G-CQDs and Asp. When Asp was added to G-CQDs for about 60 s, a stable platform with the lowest quenching value was reached. As shown in Fig. 5 (c), the fluorescence at 525 nm gradually decreased with increasing Asp concentration, indicating that Asp can effectively burst the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs, and a good linearity in the range of 1–10 μM (R2 = 0.9912) and 10–80 μM (R2 = 0.9867) can be seen in Fig. 5 (d), which was calculated as The minimum detection concentration was calculated to be 0.11 μM (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of G-CQDs samples (N = 20) and S is the slope of the calibration curve)[32,33]. In addition, compared with some fluorescent probes, the strategy of G-CQDs for Asp detection in this paper is better than some related Asp detection methods, and the sensing performance of some different fluorescent probes for Asp detection is summarized and compared in Table S1
[34–35].

In order to investigate the single selectivity of G-CQDs, various potential interfering amino acids were added to the G-CQDs solution, including Glu, Cys, Trp, Tyr, Pro, Met, His, Ile, Lys, Phe and Ser. As shown in Fig. S5 , the fluorescence was still burst when Asp was added to the G-CQDs in addition to other interfering amino acids, and the fluorescence was still burst. Even if there are potential interfering substances, Asp can effectively burst the fluorescence of G-CQDs. Therefore, G-CQDs can be used as a single fluorescent probe for Asp detection.

In general, fluorescence bursts originate from the following mechanisms: static burst (SQE), dynamic burst (DQE), internal filtering effect (IFE), aggregated fluorescence burst (ACQ), and photo-induced electron transfer (PET). Static bursts are reported to occur when the fluorescent substance interacts with the burster to form a stable non-fluorescent ground state complex, which makes the fluorescence intensity decrease. Among them, the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complexes leads to changes in the UV absorption spectrum, which is reflected, among other things, by the fact that the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs is largely unaffected. Dynamic burst, in contrast to static burst, is characterized by a constant UV absorption spectrum and a change in fluorescence lifetime[36].

In order to investigate the burst mechanism of Asp detection by G-CQDs, the changes of fluorescence lifetime and UV–vis spectra before and after the addition of Asp were investigated. In Fig. 6
(a), it can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs was 6.61 ns, and with the addition of Asp, the fluorescence lifetime was 5.35 ns and the fluorescence lifetime was changed. In Fig. 6 (b), it can be seen that the UV–vis spectra of G-CQDs and G-CQDs + Asp have peaks at 263 nm, 437 nm and 466 nm, and the positions and intensities remain basically the same. The above data suggest that the fluorescence burst of G-CQDs is caused by the dynamic burst mechanism after the addition of Asp.

In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation can be used to calculate the Kq burst rate constant to verify whether the process is a dynamic burst:
F 0
/ F = 1 + K s v
[ Q ]
Kq = K s v /
τ 0
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the burster, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, [Q] is the burster concentration, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs. When Kq is much less than 2.0*1010 L/mol. s is the dynamic burst. Substituting the corresponding data into the above equation gives Kq = 4.92*107 L/mol. s, which is much smaller than 2.0*1010 L/mol. s, it can be verified that the detection of Asp by G-CQDs is indeed due to dynamic burst[37]. The detection of Asp by G-CQDs is indeed caused by the dynamic burst.

In order to verify the feasibility of the G-CQDs in the detection of Asp in real life, the actual sample detection was carried out in diluted human urine to explore the potential application of the G-CQDs sensing probe platform in the detection of Asp in human urine samples. The unknown Asp concentration in urine samples was determined by standard addition method, and the results are shown in Table S2 . It can be seen from the table that the recovery of Asp in urine samples was between 97.25 and 105.50 %, and RSD (n = 3) was between 1.51% and 3.31%. These results reveal the high efficiency and reliability of the G-CQDs sensing platform in the determination of Asp in human urine samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Yellow fluorescent YN-CQDs for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The abundant functional groups on the surface of YN-CQDs provide potential sensing applications, and the detection of ions by fluorescent probes is mainly based on the change of fluorescence intensity. To evaluate the specificity of the YN-CQDs fluorescent sensor in detecting Fe3+, 19 ions were added to YN-CQDs, including Mg2+, Mn2+, Li+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Sr2+, Hg2+, Al3+, and Cr3+ were added to YN-CQDs to analyze their fluorescence intensity. In Fig. 7
(a) and S6(a), it can be seen that Fe3+ has the strongest fluorescence bursting ability on YN-CQDs, and the rest of the ions do not have a large effect on the fluorescence of YN-CQDs, showing specific detection with the specificity of detecting Fe3+. Subsequently, the linear concentration of Fe3+ was detected by adding 10 μg/mL of YN-CQDs as the main solution, and 30 μL of Fe3+ in the concentration range from 0 to 250 μM to the main solution, and the fluorescence intensity was analyzed whether the YN-CQDs had good linearity in the detection of Fe3+. In Fig. 7 (b), the relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration was shown. With the increase of Fe3+ concentration, the fluorescence of YN-CQDs at 585 nm began to weaken, and there was a good linear relationship in the range of 1–10 μM (R2 = 0.9839) and 10–100 μM (R2 = 0.9957) in Fig. 7 (c). The minimum detection concentration was calculated to be 0.29 μM (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of YN-CQDs samples (N = 20), S is the slope of the calibration curve)[38].

To further investigate the specificity of YN-CQDs for Fe3+ detection, experimental tests were conducted in the presence of potential interfering substances. Fig. 7 (d) and S6(b) shows the effect on the fluorescence burst of YN-CQDs when Fe3+ was added to YN-CQDs in addition to the potential interfering substance, tested under the same conditions (The concentration of Fe3+ was 0.1 mM and the concentration of the other interfering ions was 1 mM). The fluorescence burst of YN-CQDs was largely absent in the absence of Fe3+, and the fluorescence burst of YN-CQDs was greatly increased and largely uniform when Fe3+ was added to both in the presence of infectious substances. This result indicates that Fe3+ can effectively burst the fluorescence of YN-CQDs even in the presence of interfering substances, and the interference is negligible. Some of the published literature on the detection of Fe3+ is summarized in Table S3 for comparative analysis.

In order to explore the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ detected by YN-CQDs, the changes of UV–vis spectra before and after adding Fe3+ were studied. The UV–vis spectrum does not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra, so the mechanism of detecting Fe3+ is not the internal filtering effect (Fig. 8
(a)). Besides, the original YN-CQDs had a peak at 500 nm (Fig. 8 (b)). After the addition of Fe3+, the UV–vis spectrum of YN-CQDs changed significantly. The peak at 500 nm disappeared, and the absorption peak intensity caused by π-π* transition at 263 nm was enhanced. The main reason is that the surface of YN-CQDs has more oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which can coordinate with the empty 3d orbital of Fe3+ and bind to each other, thus destroying the conjugated structure. The peak at 500 nm disappears and a non-fluorescent ground state complex is formed to quench the fluorescence. Due to the change of UV–vis spectrum, the mechanism of detecting Fe3+ is not dynamic quenching, which indicates that the detection of Fe3+ by YN-CQDs is not internal filtering effect, nor dynamic quenching, but static quenching[39–40].

In order to evaluate the applicability of YN-CQDs fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection in real samples, the potential application of YN-CQDs sensing probes for detecting Fe3+ in real samples was investigated by using the standard addition method for real samples in laboratory water and treated river water. As shown in Table S4 , the recoveries of Fe3+ recoveries in real water samples ranged from 97.66 to 101.83% with RSD (n = 3) between 1.36 and 3.15%. These results reveal the high efficiency and reliability of the YN-CQDs-based fluorescent probes in the determination of Fe3+ in real samples.

A variety of fluorescent carbon quantum dots were prepared by hydrothermal method, and green fluorescent G-CQDs and nitrogen doped yellow fluorescent YN-CQDs with stable fluorescence properties were successfully obtained using PTCDA as precursor. The emission wavelength is increased by doping nitrogen. Fluorescent probes were constructed for different purposes based on the different properties of the quantum dots, including kits for pH visualization and detection, detection of Asp and Fe3+ probes, enabling versatile applications of carbon dots. This synthetic strategy provides a new idea for the preparation of other fluorescent probes with long emission wavelengths and multi-purpose applications.

Rui Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology. Simin He: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology. Wanjing Liu: Visualization. Hebing Pei: Visualization. Nijuan Liu: Supervision, Visualization. Ruibin Guo: Supervision, Visualization. Zunli Mo: Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21861034); the Science and Technology Project of Gansu Province, China (20YF3GA022); the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (18JR3RA094).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114967.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
